coredata.dc:title: Studies on a novel method for the determination of nitrosamines in food by HPLC-UV-FLD coupling with terbium-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Zhiqin
Cai, Mengdie
Gan, Weimin
Yuan, Ping
Wei, Lijun
Cheng, Xianglei
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel, selective and sensitive high performance liquid chromatography-UV photolysis-fluorescence detection (HPLC-UV-FLD) method for nitrosamines (NAs) is proposed based on terbium-doped carbon dots (Tb-CDs). Experimental studies have shown that nitrosamines can be degraded by UV photolysis with wavelength at 254 nm to produce NO2 −, while NO2 − can rapidly and specifically quench the fluorescence of Tb-CDs. Based on this principle, N-nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA), Nnitrosomorpholine (NMor), N-nitrosopyrrolidine (NPYR), N-nitrosodiethylamine (NDEA) and N-nitrosopiperidine (NPIP) are separated within 8 min by HPLC. These five kinds of NAs showed a good linear relationship in the range of 10–2000 μg/L, and the limit of detection was as low as 2.25 μg/L. The proposed method is successfully applied to the detection of sweet potato, lotus root, potato, water chestnut, tomato and cucumber. The sample recoveries range from 77.35 % to 119.3 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrosamines
HPLC
UV photolysis
Fluorescence
Terbium-dopped carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism on fluorescence recognition of UV photolysis products from NAs by Tb-CDs
Optimization ofthe variables in the proposed HPLC-UV-FLD method

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank for the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 82173574, 81960600 and 81760601), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (Nos. 20192BAB205089 and 20192BAB205091) and Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates of Jiangxi Province (No. YC2021-S071).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (A) and the inset shows the photos of CDs solutions under natural light and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. Infrared spectra of CDs and Tb-CDs (B). Elemental mapping images of C, O and Tb elements in Tb-CDs (C, D and E).
The fluorescence spectra of different solutions. Tb-CDs (A); UV degradation solution of NDEA + Tb-CDs (B); Tb3+ solution (C). Conditions: 80 mg/L CDs, 1 mM Tb3+, 100 μg/L NDEA.
Typical standard chromatograms of the studied NAs. The concentration of NDMA is 60 μg/L, and the concentration of other NAs is 120 μg/L.
Comparison of performance on the detection of NAs with different methods.

row.entry: N-nitrosomethylethylamine, N-nitrosodinpropylamine, N-nitrosodi-butylamine, N-nitrosodiphenylamine, NDEA, NMor, NPIP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of NAs in different plants.

head.ce:title: Studies on a novel method for the determination of nitrosamines in food by HPLC-UV-FLD coupling with terbium-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Preventive Medicine, School of Public Health, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Preventive Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Preventive Medicine, School of Public Health, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Public Health, Nanchang University, 461 Bayi Road, Nanchang, 330006, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel, selective and sensitive high performance liquid chromatography-UV photolysis-fluorescence detection (HPLC-UV-FLD) method for nitrosamines (NAs) is proposed based on terbium-doped carbon dots (Tb-CDs). Experimental studies have shown that nitrosamines can be degraded by UV photolysis with wavelength at 254 nm to produce NO2 −, while NO2 − can rapidly and specifically quench the fluorescence of Tb-CDs. Based on this principle, N-nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA), Nnitrosomorpholine (NMor), N-nitrosopyrrolidine (NPYR), N-nitrosodiethylamine (NDEA) and N-nitrosopiperidine (NPIP) are separated within 8 min by HPLC. These five kinds of NAs showed a good linear relationship in the range of 10–2000 μg/L, and the limit of detection was as low as 2.25 μg/L. The proposed method is successfully applied to the detection of sweet potato, lotus root, potato, water chestnut, tomato and cucumber. The sample recoveries range from 77.35 % to 119.3 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since modern times, human production activities such as industrial production, agricultural activities and domestic sewage discharge have had a significant impact on nitrogen migration and nitrogen cycle in the ecological environment and further destroyed the balance of the ecosystem (Grant, Azizian, Cook, Boano, & Rippy, 2018). Direct intake of nitrate can be converted into nitrite in the digestive tract, which can be combined with amines to form carcinogenic nitrosamines (NAs) (Suzuki, et al., 2003). In addition, excessive use of NAs precursors, or unreasonable food cooking methods (Drabik-Markiewicz, et al., 2011), and even production processes such as drinking water disinfection and water treatment (Brienza, Manasfi, Sauvetre, & Chiron, 2020; Dong, et al., 2022; C. Zhao, et al., 2022b) can produce uncontrollable NAs, resulting in NAs found in soil (Reny, Plumlee, Kodamatani, Suffet, & Roback, 2021), water (B. Zhao, et al., 2022a), meat products (Niklas, Herrmann, Pedersen, Jakobsen, & Duedahl-Olesen, 2022), drugs (Yamamoto, et al., 2022), tobacco smoke (Soleimani, Dobaradaran, De-la-Torre, Schmidt, & Saeedi, 2022) and cosmetics (Miralles, van Gernert, Chisvert, & Salvador, 2019). Recently, Li et al (X. Li, et al., 2021) reported that the main contributors to N-nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA), N-nitrosodiethylamine (NDEA) and total volatile NAs intakes of residents from 20 provinces in China were vegetables and cereals. Moreover, another literature (Coffacci, Codognoto, Fleuri, Lima, & Pedrosa, 2013) found that the contents of NAs of vegetables from organic farming were less than that from conventionally grown products. They concluded that, in conventional methods, nitrogen was added to fertilizer to be made available to plants for protein production. Therefore, the research and establishment on reliable methods for the detection of NAs pollutants in environment and food related to human health is of great importance to ensure and maintain the health and safety of human beings.

Due to the variety of chemical properties from different NAs (Vrzal & Olsovska, 2019), it is difficult to simultaneously detect and analyze NAs by using a single analytical strategy. At present, the methods for the determination of NAs mainly focus on gas chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (GC–MS/MS) (Xian, et al., 2019), liquid chromatography and liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Iammarino, Mangiacotti, & Chiaravalle, 2019; Roth-Konforti, et al., 2019), fluorescence (Hu, Liu, Li, Chen, & Wu, 2018), chemiluminescence (Breider & von Gunten, 2017) and so on. LC-MS/MS has the advantages of high accuracy and good selectivity. However, in order to obtain a lower detection limit, different NAs must use different ion sources, which brings a lot of inconvenience to the practical application. In addition, with the disadvantages of expensive instrument price, high maintenance cost and more energy consumption, both GC–MS/MS and LC-MS/MS are not suitable to meet the challenge of global warming. Compared with other methods, fluorescence analysis has the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, energy saving and high instrument popularization. It has been widely used in chemistry (J. J. Liu, Li, & Yang, 2020), food (Duan, Yang, Tian, & Sun, 2021; G. L. Li, Zhang, Zheng, Liu, & Wu, 2020), environment (Bryden & Zysman-Colman, 2021), life science (Yin, et al., 2021), medicine (Cheng, et al., 2022), material science (Fan, et al., 2021) and other interdisciplinary fields. However, NAs are instinctively non-fluorescent compounds. In order to achieve the detection of NAs, some procedures by the addition of hydrobromic acid and acetic acid (Zheng, Fu, & Xu, 1993) were adopted to transfer NAs into corresponding secondary amines. Then the secondary amines were further reacted to generate fluorescent products through pre-column derivatization. On the other hand, the derivatization process is time-consuming and inefficient. Moreover, because of inherent fluorescence, the derivative reagents inevitably interfere with the detection of NAs. Therefore, interesting challenges for the development of new fluorescence detection systems have been still encouraged.

As an advanced oxidation processes, UV photolysis technology has shown great advantages in effectively removing organic pollutants in water. UV photolysis is favored by environmental and chemical researchers (Ma, 2017; Zhu, et al., 2017) because of its simple structure, free for chemical reagents, stability, good reproducibility and so on. In the previous literatures (Alizadeh & Farokhcheh, 2014; Lu, et al., 2017; Zhao, et al., 2016), HPLC-FLD method for the determination of NAs are setpu based on the use of dansylation reaction to label the secondary amine produced by UV photolysis of NAs. Unfortunately, these dansylating reagents are easy to react with coexisting amines in the samples at the same time resulting in NAs overestimation. Hence, the existing researches are lack of innovative ideas.

In recent years, the rapid development of nanomaterials, especially carbon dots (CDs), provides a new opportunity for luminescence analysis and shows a broad application prospect (J. J. Liu, Li, & Yang, 2020). However, less researches (Hu, Liu, Li, Chen, & Wu, 2018) on quantum dot materials are used in NAs and the limit of detection (LODs) is too high to be used for the detection of NAs in actual samples.

In our preliminary studies, it was found that the UV-degraded NDEA could obviously quench the fluorescence of the synthesized terbium-dopped carbon dots (Tb-CDs). Taking this phenomenon as the starting point, this study is intended to firstly reveal the generation of intermediates by UV photolysis of NAs, and then elucidate the quenching mechanism of specific fluorescence of Tb-CDs by intermediates. Furthermore, the simultaneously on-line analysis of multiple NAs by means of HPLC-UV-FLD with significantly selectivity. The performance of the proposed method was evaluated for the detection of NAs in various food samples. The present study reveals a new light on the development of methods for the determination of NAs in foods.

Ultrapure water was purified using a Direct-Q5 UV ultrapure water system (18.2 MΩ cm; Millipore). Nicotinic acid, barbituric acid, NDMA, Nnitrosomorpholine (NMor), N-nitrosopyrrolidine (NPYR), NDEA and N-nitrosopiperidine (NPIP) were all purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Stock solutions (1 g/L) of NAs were prepared by dissolving the corresponding compounds in ultrapure water. NAs solutions with the concentration from 10 μg/L to 1000 μg/L were prepared by diluting the stock solutions with ultrapure water. All solutions were stored in the dark at 4 ℃. Terbium nitrate hexahydrate was from Macklin biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetonitrile was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The chromatographic system consisting of a Waters 2695 HPLC equipment, a thermostat column compartment and a 2475 fluorescence detector are all manufactured by Waters Technologies (USA). The excitation and emission spectra were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-320, Tianjin Gangdong Technology Co., Ltd.).

The schematic diagram of HPLC-UV-FLD is shown in Fig. 1 . A isocratic elution program is used with the mobile phase (A:B, 85:15, V:V) by combining solvent A (water) and solvent B (acetonitrile). NAs are separated at 55 ℃ with a flow rate of 1.5 mL/min. The injected volume is 100 μL and all compounds are eluted within 8.0 min. The excitation and emission wavelengths of fluorescence detection are set at 280 nm and 541 nm, respectively. The chromatographic column is ZORBAX SB-C18 (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5.0 μm). The post-column device designed for UV photolysis reaction (Aura Industries Co. ltd) is installed between the chromatographic column and the fluorescence detector, which is composed of a 254 nm low-pressure mercury lamp (150 W) and a transparent PTFE tube around the UV lamp. Tb-CDs solution is transported by precision constant current pump (CP-M210, CoMetro Co. ltd., USA) at the flow rate of 0.9 mL/min.

In this experiment, CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with slight modification on the work of Wu and Tong (Wu & Tong, 2020). The basic steps were as follows: 369.0 mg nicotinic acid and 384.0 mg barbituric acid are dissolved in 20 mL ultra-pure water. Then the mixed solution was transferred to 100 mL autoclave after ultrasonic for 15 min and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. After natural cooling, the yellowish solution was centrifuged for 15 min (10000 rpm) and filtered with a 0.45 μm filter to remove small molecules. The obtained carbon dots solution was freeze-dried to obtain carbon dots powder. Tb-CDs solution was obtained by dissolving appreciate amounts of carbon dots and terbium nitrate in ultra-pure water.

All samples were purchased from local markets. Sweet potatoes, roots, potatoes and water chestnuts were washed, peeled and cut into pieces. While cucumbers and tomatoes were directly cut into pieces after being washed. The pieces of samples were further crushed and then freeze-dried. The dried samples were ground into powder by mortar. About 0.5 g (accurate to 0.1 mg) was weighed, mixed with 4 mL ultrapure water and then spiked into three different kinds of concentration of NAs (0.20 μg/g, 0.40 μg/g, 0.80 μg/g). The mixture was vortexed for 30 s, and extracted by ultrasonic for 10 min. Then 3 mL × 2 ultrapure water was added to repeat the above steps again. The above extracts were combined and filtered to remove the residue, then filtered through 0.22 μm membrane. The resulted solutions were kept at 4 ℃ until analysis. Recovery was calculated as the percentage of the found mass of the analyte on the spiked sample toward the mass that was initially spiked.

The synthesized CDs were characterized by high resolution field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM2100F, JEOL, Japan). As shown in Fig. 2
A, these carbon dots are spherical and uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of 1.3 ± 0.6 nm. The prepared CDs were characterized by an infrared spectrometer (IR, IS50, Thermofisher, USA), as shown in Fig. 2 B. The results indicated that CDs had typical C O absorption (1182 cm−1 and 1086 cm−1) and COOH absorption (2395 cm−1). In addition, CDs also shows all kinds of vibrations of N H, CO, CC/CN and C N at 3071 cm−1, 1698 cm−1, 1594 cm−1 and 1317 cm−1, respectively. In the IR spectra of Tb-CDs, as shown in Fig. 2 B (red line), the characteristic absorption of COOH almost disappeared and the absorption peak of CO decreased obviously. Moreover, the characteristic absorption peak of CN shifted from 1317 cm−1 to 1397 cm−1. With the help of electron microscope energy spectrum analysis (X-max80T, Oxford instrument), the element distribution map of Tb-CDs was obtained (Fig. 3
C–E). According to the data, the contents of C, O and Tb elements were 76.04 % 12.90 % 11.06 % respectively. Based on these data, it is speculated that there must be coordination effect between Tb3+ and carboxyl/amine groups on the surface of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism on fluorescence recognition of UV photolysis products from NAs by Tb-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Studies (Kaur, Chaudhary, Jena, & Singh, 2020; Y. Li, Liu, Wang, Wang, & Qiu, 2021) have shown that rare earth metals can receive energy transferring from quantum dots through antenna effect to produce strong rare earth metal characteristic fluorescence. The self-fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ was weak (Fig. 3 C), and the fluorescence intensity increased about 10 times when mixed with CDs (Fig. 3 A). If NDEA was directly mixed with Tb-CDs solution, no change on Tb-CDs fluorescence was found (not shown here). However, when NDEA was degraded by ultraviolet light with the wavelength at 254 nm, the fluorescence intensity of Tb-CDs was significantly reduced by the degraded NDEA solution (Fig. 3 B). This indicated that the product of ultraviolet photolysis of NDEA could interact with Tb-CDs. Some reports (Min, et al., 2020; Wu & Tong, 2020) have shown that NAs can be degraded into different kinds of products, such as secondary amines, NO2 − and NO3 −, under ultraviolet light with the wavelength at 254 nm. In order to clarify the photolysis products of NDEA in the present work, ion chromatography was employed to determinate the UV photolysis solution of NDEA. The results showed that 100.0 μg/L NDEA could produce 25.0 μg/L, 20.0 μg/L and 13.9 μg/L NO2 − in aqueous solution, 15 % acetonitrile solution and 20 % methanol solution, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence of Tb-CDs changed with different kinds of photolysis solvents. And the quenching efficiency of Tb-CDs reduced according to the following list, water, 15 % acetonitrile, and 20 % methanol. Thus, it is speculated that the decrease in the fluorescence of Tb-CDs might be associated with nitrite. Fortunately, a recent study has confirmed that trace NO2 − can quench the fluorescence of Tb3+. In conclusion, NAs could be degraded by UV light resulting in the product NO2 −. While NO2 − could reduce the fluorescence intensity of Tb-CDs. Then NAs could be recognized by Tb-CDs with the photolysis intermediate NO2 − as a bridge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization ofthe variables in the proposed HPLC-UV-FLD method
ce:section.ce:para: There are many parameters involved in the construction of the present HPLC-UV-FLD system. So, the type of chromatographic column, the composition of mobile phase, the column temperature, flow rate of chromatographic separation system, the wavelength of ultraviolet lamp, the length of knotted coil, flow rate of precision pump, the concentration of CDs and terbium nitrate were optimized successively in the experiments.

First of all, the separation capabilities of Zorbax SB-C18 column (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm), XBridge C18 column (4.6 mm × 150 mm, 3.5 μm) and Kinetex C18 column (4.6 mm × 100 mm, 2.6 μm) were investigated in this work. Due to the strong polarity, NDMA, NPIP and NMor were eluted from the column at dead time regardless of the ratio of organic phases, and could not be retained on both XBridgeC18 and KinetexC18 columns. As for SB-C18 column, five studied NAs could be separated completely in a wide range of the percent organic in the mobile phase. Therefore, the Zorbax SB-C18 column was used in the follow-up experiment.

Secondly, the effects of composition on the separation of NAs were investigated with buffer solution, pure water, methanol-water, acetonitrile-water or methanol-acetonitrile-water mixed solvents as the mobile phase. The results showed that methanol-water, acetonitrile-water and methanol-acetonitrile-water mixed solvents could realize the baseline resolution of the target NAs. However, when methanol was contained in the mobile phase, the fluorescence sensitivity of Tb-CDs would decrease when encountering with photolysis products of NAs, which might be related to the consumption of hydroxyl radicals stemming from UV photolysis procedures. As a result, the addition of methanol leads to the reduction of NO2 − production. Then, the effect of 10 % to 50 % acetonitrile as the mobile phase was further discussed on the separation of five kinds of NAs. Although the separation efficiency was higher when the acetonitrile proportion was more than 20 %, the influence of baseline noise and drift turned worse. Consequently, 15 % acetonitrile was selected as the mobile phase in the successive experiments.

It was found that the increase of temperature was beneficial to the photolysis of NAs. As shown in Fig. S1A, when the column temperature increased, the detection sensitivity of this method was higher. It was speculated that the increase of temperature favored the cleavage of NN bonds in NAs and promoted the formation of NO2 −. However, since the SB-C18 column particles consist of a silica support and a bonded organic surface layer C18, the lifespan of the column would be rapidly reduced if the temperature was too high. So, 55 ℃ was chosen as the best separation temperature in this experiment.

In HPLC analysis, the flow rate has great influence on the separation efficiency of analytes. The effect of the flow rate ranging from 0.9 to 1.8 mL/min on the separation of five NAs compounds was investigated. When the flow rate was lower than 1.5 mL/min, the analytes could be well separated. Both the separation efficiency and signal intensity increased with the increasing of flow rate. When the flow rate was higher than 1.5 mL/min, although the separation efficiency was improved, the band resolutions between the former three kinds of NAs became worse. Therefore, 1.5 mL/min was selected as the best flow rate for chromatographic separation.

For the post-column UV photolysis system, the effects of UV lamps with different wavelengths (365 nm, 254 nm) and knotted coils with different lengths on NAs degradation were studied. The results revealed that the photolysis products had significant effects on Tb-CDs fluorescence when UV with wavelength of 254 nm was employed. As shown in Fig. S1B, except for NMor, other NAs achieved the best results when the length of knotted coils was 1 m. Furthermore, the retention time kept almost constant when the length of knotted coil was 0.5 m and 1 m, respectively. While the baseline signals were more stable when the length was 1 m, so the UV lamp with the wavelength at 254 nm and the length of knotted coil of 1 m were selected. As illustrated in Fig. S1B (black column), if the UV lamp was turned off, there was not any change in the fluorescence signal, thus it could be explained that UV light played an important role in NAs photolysis.

The precision pump was used to deliver the solution of Tb-CDs, therefore the flow rate of precision pump was of great importance for the fluorescence of Tb-CDs and the sensitivity and accuracy of the proposed method. As shown in Fig. S1C, when the flow rate of precision pump was 0.9 mL/min, all the concerned NAs obtained the best performance on the fluorescence changes. After that, the difference of fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly. This phenomena implied that the interaction between Tb-CDs and NO2 − needed a few seconds. Thus, 0.9 mL/min was chosen as the suitable flow rate of precision pump for further experiment.

As for the concentration of CDs, it was quite vital to the optimization of the present method. As illustrated in Fig. S1D, when the content of CDs was lower than 80 mg/L, the quenching efficiency of NAs solution after photolysis increased rapidly. On the other hand, when the concentration of CDs was higher than 80 mg/L, the fluorescence changes decreased slowly. Similarly, the concentration of 1 mM Tb3+ was selected. Then Tb-CDs was synthesized under the condition of 1 mM Tb3+ and 80 mg/L CDs.

The method was validated according to the 2002/657/EC Decision (European Commission, 2002). Under the optimal conditions described above, the concerned NAs were separated within 8 min and a typical chromatogram was shown in Fig. 4 . Calibration curves were obtained by the standard solution of NAs covering the whole linear and each point in triplicate. Additionally, the analytical figure of merit was shown in Table S1. The five studied NAs showed good linearity in the range of 10–2000 μg/L with the LOD (S/N = 3) as low as 2.25 μg/L and relative standard deviations (RSD) ranging from 1.71 % to 3.64. The LOD of different methods for the detection of NAs were summarized in Table 1 . The comparative results showed that the LOD of the present HPLC-UV-FLD method was superior to other LC-FLD (Cha, Fox, & Nalinakumari, 2006), LC-UV/Vis (Chisvert, Benedé, Peiró, Pedrón, & Salvador, 2017), CE-MS(X. Liu, et al., 2007) methods and comparable to LC-MS/MS (Z. Li, et al., 2019) and GC–MS/MS (J. Liu, et al., 2021) methods. Furthermore, the proposed method enabled direct sample analysis without pre-treatment such as solid phase extraction and liquid-liquid extraction. This indicated that the established HPLC-UV-FLD offered an alternative approach for the ultrasensitive detection of NAs with high selectivity and powerful contamination immunity.

The evaluation of the intraday and interday precision and accuracy of the assay, cucumber samples with NAs at 0.200, 0.400 and 0.800 ng/g concentrations were carried out as described above. The RSD of intraday ranged from 1.67 to 4.56 % in quintuplicate, while the interday RSD ranged from 3.30 to 6.43 % in three consecutive days. The results demonstrated good repeatability and accuracy within the concentration range selected. Recoveries were calculated by comparison of peak areas from spiked samples with the standard solutions of equivalent concentrations. The mean recoveries of five studied NAs were in the range of 81.2–112.5 %. Therefore, the developed method was suitable for the determination of NAs residues in vegetables.

In order to test the feasibility of the method in practical application, six food samples of sweet potato, lotus root, potato, water chestnut, tomato and cucumber were selected for analysis in this study (Table 2 ). The results showed that the sample recoveries ranged from 77.35 % to 119.30 %. Among the six actual samples tested, NAs were detected in five samples. Among them, NDMA was found in four samples and NMor was detected in one sample (water chestnut). It is not difficult to understand that the four samples of sweet potatoes, roots, potatoes and water chestnuts could continuously absorb the NAs formed in the soil or mud because they are buried in soil or mud for a long time, resulting in the presence of NAs in the samples. Tomatoes are fruits of plants growing on the ground, which are far away from the underground root system, so the level of NAs detected in tomatoes is low. In contrast, cucumbers are fruits from vine plants and the fruits are further away from the root system. Hence, NAs from the soil are not transported to cucumbers.

A novel, selective, sensitive and on-line HPLC-UV-FLD approach for the determination of five kinds of NAs was established based on Tb-CDs in this paper. After post-column UV photolysis, NAs were transformed into the intermediate NO2 −, which could specifically quench the fluorescence produced by Tb-CDs. The LOD of NAs was as low as 2.25 μg/L. The method was successfully applied to the detection of actual plant samples and the recoveries ranged between 77.35 % and 119.30 %.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank for the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 82173574, 81960600 and 81760601), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (Nos. 20192BAB205089 and 20192BAB205091) and Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates of Jiangxi Province (No. YC2021-S071).

Talanta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantification of total N-nitrosamine concentrations in aqueous samples via UV-photolysis and chemiluminescence detection of nitric oxide
Anal. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) compounds used in photocatalysis
Chem. Soc. Rev.

Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Cheng et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-color fluorescent probe allows simultaneous imaging of main and papain-like proteases of SARS-CoV-2-infected cells for accurate detection and rapid inhibitor screening
